questions: At **CyanGate LLC**, you led the development of the **JSON-to-JSON AI Agentic System**, which integrated multiple LLM providers like OpenAI, Copilot, and Gemini—only to later see new model releases or framework shifts (e.g., MCP Server Integration) disrupt prior work. Walk me through a specific instance where a major technical or business pivot forced you to abandon or radically rework a solution you’d invested significant time in. How did you manage your own frustration, communicate the change to stakeholders, and what did you learn about balancing innovation with stability in fast-moving AI projects?

Your **Atlassian Jira Intelligence System** project at CyanGate required close collaboration with non-technical stakeholders to translate their needs into an AI-powered Teams assistant. Imagine halfway through the project, leadership suddenly decided to reprioritize the team toward a completely different use case (e.g., shifting from Jira analytics to a **health data processing system**). Describe how you would handle this scenario: How would you realign your technical approach, manage the emotional impact on your team, and ensure the new direction still delivered value without losing momentum?

You’ve competed in high-pressure environments like **TEKNOFEST**, where your team secured a **Bronze Medal in the E-Commerce AI Competition** by rapidly iterating on a multimodal system. Now, suppose you joined Zephlen and were assigned to a project where, after weeks of development, user testing revealed your agentic workflow solution was fundamentally misaligned with real-world needs—requiring a complete architectural overhaul. How would you react in the moment? What steps would you take to diagnose the root cause (e.g., flawed assumptions, poor stakeholder alignment), and how would you propose a path forward while maintaining trust with both your team and leadership?
